g.data-narration: Contract Management is essential for software projects to define scope, deliverables, timelines, and legal obligations between parties.
Contract management creates formal agreements between clients and vendors, ensuring clear expectations and legal protection for software projects.
g.text: Software projects involve multiple stakeholders, offshore teams, and third-party vendors.
Well-structured contracts provide clarity, risk mitigation, and successful project outcomes.

g.data-narration: This topic covers contract types, lifecycle phases, SLAs, key elements, and vendor management practices essential for software professionals.
Three primary contract types exist in software projects: Fixed Price, Time and Materials, and Cost-Plus. Each allocates risk differently between parties.
g.text: Types of Contracts in Software Projects

g.data-narration: Fixed Price contracts set a predetermined price for the entire project. The vendor assumes cost overrun risk, making it suitable for well-defined requirements.
g.text: • Vendor bears risk of cost overruns - budget certainty for client
• Suitable for well-defined projects with clear requirements
• Risk: May lead to scope creep or lower quality if vendor faces financial pressure

g.data-narration: Time and Materials contracts pay for actual time spent and materials used. This transfers cost risk to the client, ideal for evolving requirements.
g.text: • Client pays based on actual time and resources used
• Flexible for projects with evolving or unclear requirements
• Risk: Cost risk transferred to client - requires good oversight

g.data-narration: Cost-Plus contracts reimburse all legitimate costs plus a fixed fee or percentage markup. Common in government projects requiring cost transparency.
g.text: • Vendor reimbursed for all legitimate costs plus fixed fee/markup
• Ensures vendor not penalized for unforeseen complexities
• Requires extensive documentation and audit capabilities

g.data-narration: The contract lifecycle has five phases: Initiation, Negotiation, Execution, Monitoring, and Closure. Each phase involves specific activities.
g.text: Each phase: Initiation→needs & scope | Negotiation→terms & pricing | Execution→signatures | Monitoring→performance | Closure→final delivery

g.data-narration: SLAs define service quality standards with metrics like uptime, response time, and resolution time. Violations result in financial penalties or service credits.
For 99.9% uptime: annual allowed downtime is 8.76 hours. With 6 hours downtime and ₹10,000/hour penalty, calculate the excess penalty.
g.text: Given: 99.9% uptime guarantee, 6 hours downtime in a month
Formula: Allowed downtime = Total hours × (100 - Uptime%) / 100

g.data-narration: A comprehensive contract must include: scope of work, deliverables, timelines, payment terms, IP rights, confidentiality, termination, and dispute resolution.
Change management handles scope modifications. The 5-step process prevents scope creep: request, impact assessment, negotiation, approval, and implementation.
Vendor management involves pre-qualification, performance reviews, relationship management, and risk assessment. Strong vendor relationships drive project success.
Key takeaways: Contract types allocate risk differently. SLAs define service standards. Always document changes. Strong contracts protect all stakeholders.
